mnemonics.title: ENUM Key Features
mnemonics.content: E-N-U-M: Enums have Natural constants (uniquely identified), Underlying values (ordinal), Methods (custom behavior), and Interfaces (can implement). Think: 'Enums Naturally Use Many Interfaces'.

mnemonics.title: Enum Class Features
mnemonics.content: Remember ENUMS: Enums are Not Just Simple constants - they have Methods, Can implement interfaces, Can have constructors (private), Can have fields, Are type-safe, Support switch statements, Can have abstract methods.

acronyms.expansion: ENumerated NUMber - a set of named constants
acronyms.usage: Used to define fixed sets like Days, Months, Status values

acronyms.expansion: Enhanced NUMeric type - replaced int constants
acronyms.usage: Type-safe replacement for public static final int constants

visualTips.visualization: Think of enum constants as objects (instances) of the enum class, like a factory that produces only a fixed number of predefined products.
Imagine each enum constant is an object being constructed in a factory. The constructor is called exactly once for each constant, like stamping out products.
Picture values() as a conveyor belt that collects all enum constants in order and delivers them as an array.
Think of ordinal as the seat number in a theater - first seat is 0, second is 1, and so on. Each enum constant has its fixed seat.

analogies.analogy: Using int constants is like using room numbers (101, 102, 103) - confusing and error-prone. Using enums is like using named rooms (Kitchen, Bedroom, Hall) - clear and meaningful.

analogies.analization: An enum is like a family with fixed members. You can describe a person as 'Johnson' but you can't add 'Smith' - the family has only its defined members.

analogies.analogy: The private constructor is like a secret recipe - only the enum itself (the chef) can create its constants, not external code.
values() is like listing all players in a team; valueOf() is like calling a player by name to get them from the team.
